content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 20, 1992


Robert G. Boehm
City Attorney
City of Chico
196 East Fifth Street
Post Office Box 3420
Chico, CA 95927

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-92-049

Dear Mr. Boehm:

This is in response to your letter requesting assistance
regarding the status of a bond counsel to the city with respect to
his or her responsibilities as a consultant under the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
Since your question does not identify a particular person or
concern a specific pending governmental decision but rather seeks
general guidance, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.2

QUESTION

Is a bond counsel who provides legal services to a city with
respect to the issuance of municipal tax-exempt bonds a
"consultant" as that term is defined in the Act for purposes of
the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION

Under the facts you have provided, a person who contracts
with a public agency to perform legal services as a bond counsel

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-92-049
Page 2


is a "consultant" (public official) within the meaning of the
Act.3

FACTS

The City of Chico, like many other public agencies in the
state, frequently hires special legal counsel to provide legal
services in connection with the issuance of municipal tax-exempt
bonds including, but not limited to, general obligation bonds,
assessment district bonds, revenue bonds, and Mello-Roos bonds.
The services they provide to the agency issuing the bonds consist
of legal advice concerning compliance with the procedural statute
or ordinance under which the bonds are issued and an opinion,
generally relied upon by the municipal bond market, that the bonds
being issued by the public agency are, in fact, tax-exempt.

Like most city attorneys and other public agency legal
counsel, you believe that you have the expertise to independently
review advice given by bond counsel in regard to compliance with
the particular procedural statute or ordinance under which the
bonds are issued. However, in your opinion that very few city
attorneys of other public agency counsel have the expertise to
review opinions on the taxability of a bond issue. For example,
you rely totally on the conclusions of bond counsel to determine
whether a bond issuance qualifies as a nontaxable obligation.

On April 2, 1992, we spoke on the telephone and you gave me
more detailed general information regarding the duties and
responsibilities of a bond counsel with respect to issuance of
assessment district bonds. This type of issuance occurs when a
city determines it wants to establish a special assessment to
finance public improvements. While plans and specifications for
the improvements are drawn up by an engineer, a determination must
be made by the city as to what statutes it elects to apply to
create the assessment district. For example, according to the
California Debt Issuance Primer (which you provided on April 7,
1992), some of the laws combine the authority to issue bonds with
the procedures necessary to establish the assessment district in
the same statute. Others only specify the procedures necessary to
establish the assessment district and incorporate by reference
another statute for the issuance of the assessment bonds. Thus,
from the outset, bond counsel works closely with the city and its
financial adviser to determine the financing and manner in which
the city elects to secure payment on the principal of the bonds.


3
The Commission will begin considering amendments to the
"consultant" regulation (Regulation 18700(a)(2)) at its July
meeting. Accordingly, the advice contained in this letter may
change as a result of those amendments. Staff invites your
comments on the proposed amendments and welcomes your
participation in this process.

Our File No. I-92-049
Page 3


     Bond counsel not only advises the city on compliance with the
statutes selected but also prepares all the documents necessary to
establish an assessment district. This includes resolutions of
intention and confirmation of the assessment, levying the assessment,
proceeding with the assessment, and authorizing the bond issuance.
In addition, bond counsel prepares a letter opinion of
qualification for the tax exempt status of the bond issuance.
Bond counsel drafts the bond covenants in conjunction with the
underwriter.

     Sometimes, though infrequently, bond counsel may be involved
in preparing the terms and provisions of the public works contract
and in awarding the contract.

     Bond counsel always works on a contingency basis. If no
bonds are issued, no compensation is paid. Bond counsel's
compensation is derived from bond revenues which ultimately come
from the property owners assessed.

     Bond counsel works closely with city officials throughout the
entire process with respect to the issuance of assessment district
bonds. Bond counsel is retained by the city to provide both his
or her expertise and oversight. Bond counsel may own property or
have an economic interest in the city which may financially
benefit by an assessment district proceeding.

                              ANALYSIS

     The Political Reform Act was enacted by the people of the
State of California by initiative in 1974. The purpose for the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act is to ensure
that public officials, whether elected or appointed, will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by
their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 8100l(b).)

     Section 82048 provides, in pertinent part, that every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency is a "public official." While all designated employees of
an agency are deemed to be "public officials" under the Act, this
is not necessarily true for consultants. (In re Morrissey (1976)
2 FPPC Ops. 120.) Consultants are considered to be "public
officials" subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Act only if they meet the definition set forth in subdivis­ion
(a)(2) of Regulation 18700.

     The term "consultant" is defined in Regulation 18700(a)(2) as
follows:

          "Consultant" shall include any natural person
     who provides under contract, information, advice,
     recommendation or counsel to a state or local
     government agency, provided, however, that
     "consultant" shall not include a person who:

Our File No. I-92-049
Page 4


(A) Conducts research and arrives at
conclusions with respect to his or her
rendition of information, advice,
recommendation, or counsel independent of
control and direction of the agency or any
agency official, other than normal contract
monitoring; and

(B) Possesses no authority with respect
to any agency decision beyond the rendition of
information, advice, recommendation or
counsel.

Regulation 18700(a)(2).

Thus, a bond counsel is a "consultant," as that term is
defined in the Act, if the bond counsel provides information,
advice, recommendation or counsel to any state or local government
agency and either is subject to the agency's control and
direction or possesses authority with respect to any agency
decisions. Conversely, if a bond counsel acts independently of
agency's control and direction and possesses no authority
with respect to any agency decisions beyond the rendition of
information, advice, recommendation or counsel, the bond counsel
is not a consultant or public official under the Act. In this
regard, note that both of the requirements of Regulation
18700(a)(2), (A) and (B), must be met for the exclusion to
operate.

Under subdivision (A) of Regulation 18700(a)(2), the
Commission has found that the exclusion does apply when
persons who contract with a government agency ("contractors"), in
essence, act like "employees" and provide general advice on an
ongoing basis with duties like those normally performed by agency
staff. In re Maloney (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69; In re Leach (1978) 4
FPPC Ops. 48; Davis Advice Letter, No. A-91-473.) On the other
hand, the exclusion does apply when persons with an
agency are retained to use their own judgment and expertise to
render professional services according to the specifications of a
contract and their decisions are not subject to day-to-day review
or direction by the agency. In those situations, the contractors
enter into a contract with the public agency to deliver a finished
product, just as any vendor of goods and services, and not to
participate in or advise the agency on general governmental
decisions requiring the subject of their expertise. (Clifford
Advice Letter, No. A-83-103.) These persons who contract with an
agency do meet the definition of "consultant" as that term is
defined in the Act. Thus, whether a bond counsel comes within the
exclusion depends in large part on the degree of the bond
counsel's independence from the public agency, in this case, the
city.

Our File No. I-92-049
Page 5


Under subdivision (B) of Regulation 18700(a)(2), the
Commission has determined that the exclusion does not apply when
persons who contract with an agency not only offer their
professional opinion on specific questions or on requests for
information regarding specific factual issues, but also engage in
actual governmental decisionmaking, such as participating in
meetings and discussions with agency staff. (In re Maloney supra;
In re Leach, supra.) Under those circumstances, the contractor's
authority may be implied. (Olsen Advice Letter, No. I-91-118.)
Furthermore, the definition of consultant does not require that
the contractor "make" a final decision. (Nelson Advice Letter,
No. I-91-437.)

Thus, Regulation 18700(a)(2) automatically sweeps into the
Act any person who has some authority to make an agency decision
rather than to merely participate in one by making recommendations
to the decisionmaker. Typically, persons who are retained by an
agency to perform studies, audits or evaluations are excluded from
the coverage of the Act because they do not make governmental
decisions and do not act as quasi-employees of the agency. (In re
Leach, supra.)

From the inception of Regulation 18700, the Commission has
recognized the distinction between the independent contractor and
the quasi-employee. In reaching a determination on this vital
issue, the Commission has concluded that it is necessary to
examine the scope of activities performed by the contractor. The
question to ask is whether the duties of the person retained by
the public agency are such as to bring them within the meaning of
the term "consultant." (Workman Advice Letter, No. I-87-078.)⁴

It is against this backdrop that we must examine your
question. First, it is manifest from your facts that bond counsel

___________________________
⁴ For example, in Maloney, the survey-engineer who acted as a
consultant for the county was found not to be a public official
when he prepared surveys and engineering studies because he was
not involved in any official decisionmaking. However, this same
person was considered a public official when he acted as a
regulator and reviewer of permit applications. (In re Maloney
(1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69.) When the scope of work of a traffic
consultant to a city included traffic studies, providing traffic
counts, and identifying necessary street designs and alternative
mitigation measures, the Commission concluded that it appeared as
though he was an independent contractor and not a consultant as
that term is defined in the Act. (Gilbert Advice Letter, No. I-
88-441.) However, when the traffic consultant performed other
tasks, such as working with city staff to identify trip generation
rates for a variety of land uses, assisting the city in
determining areas of benefit within the study area, and commenting
on alternative financing techniques to distribute costs, the
Commission opined that he may be considered a consultant within
the meaning of the Act.

Our File No. I-92-049
Page 6


does not act independently of the city's control and direction.
Bond counsel appears to work closely with city staff and, in fact,
functions in an oversight capacity. Thus, the requirement for
implementation of the exclusion set forth in subdivision (A) of
Regulation 18700(a)(2) has not been met.

Secondly, it is clear from your facts that bond counsel has
broad discretion and actively participates in the city's
decisionmaking process. Not only does bond counsel participate in
the city's initial decision to select the statutes to apply and
the method of financing, but bond counsel participates in all city
decisions concerning the establishment of the assessment district.
In fact, some cities involve the bond counsel in the public works
contract. Thus, the requirement for implementation of the
exclusion set forth in subdivision (B) of Regulation 18700(a)(2)
also has not been met.

In view of the foregoing, we conclude that a person who
contracts with a public agency to perform services as a bond
counsel is a "consultant" (public official) within the meaning of
the Act.

You suggest in your letter that regardless of the definition
of "consultant," since bond counsel is retained to provide
expertise with respect to a specific project, the issuance of
municipal tax-exempt bonds, and not to advise and consult
generally, bond counsel should not be treated as a consultant
under the Act. The Commission has never enunciated a rule that
excludes consultants from coverage under the Act on the basis that
they have been retained only for a specific project. Though it is
accurate to state that the retention of a consultant under
contract for a specific project is a factor we consider in our
examination of whether a consultant is a public official under the
Act, the Commission has never held that this factor is
determinative. 5

Despite the fact that bond counsel is retained to provide
legal services in connection with only one project, counsel's
close working relationship with the city plus counsel's
decisionmaking role make it clear that under the facts presented,
bond counsel is functioning as a quasi-employee, not as an


5    For an in-depth discussion on this point, we refer you to the
Davis Advice Letter, supra at pgs. 9-10, a copy of which is
enclosed herewith.)

Our File No. I-92-049
Page 7


independent contractor, of the city.6 As such, he is a consultant
(public official) under the Act and will have to be designated in
the city's conflict of interest code.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[handwritten signature: Deanne Stone]

By: Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DRS:aa

Enclosure


6    If, for example, bond counsel is retained by the city for the
sole purpose of drafting tax documents such as the letter opinion
of qualification for tax exempt status of the bond issuance, then
bond counsel would be functioning as an independent contractor of
the city and the exclusionary requirements of Regulation
18700(a)(2)(A) and (B) would not be met. In such a case, bond counsel
would not be considered a "consultant" as that term is defined in
the Act.

7    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question: Is a bond counsel who provides legal services to a city with respect to the issuance of municipal tax-exempt bonds a "consultant" as that term is defined in the Act for purposes of the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?

Under the facts you have provided, a person who contracts with a public agency to perform legal services as a bond counsel

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

is a "consultant" (public official) within the meaning of the Act.3
sections.facts:
The City of Chico, like many other public agencies in the state, frequently hires special legal counsel to provide legal services in connection with the issuance of municipal tax-exempt bonds including, but not limited to, general obligation bonds, assessment district bonds, revenue bonds, and Mello-Roos bonds. The services they provide to the agency issuing the bonds consist of legal advice concerning compliance with the procedural statute or ordinance under which the bonds are issued and an opinion, generally relied upon by the municipal bond market, that the bonds being issued by the public agency are, in fact, tax-exempt.

Like most city attorneys and other public agency legal counsel, you believe that you have the expertise to independently review advice given by bond counsel in regard to compliance with the particular procedural statute or ordinance under which the bonds are issued. However, it is your opinion that very few city attorneys or other public agency counsel have the expertise to review opinions on the taxability of a bond issue. For example, you rely totally on the conclusions of bond counsel to determine whether a bond issuance qualifies as a nontaxable obligation.

On April 2, 1992, we spoke on the telephone and you gave me more detailed generic information regarding the duties and responsibilities of a bond counsel with respect to issuance of assessment district bonds. This type of issuance occurs when a city determines it wants to establish a special assessment to finance public improvements. While plans and specifications for the improvements are drawn up by an engineer, a determination must be made by the city as to what statutes it elects to apply to create the assessment district. For example, according to the California Debt Issuance Primer (which you provided on April 7, 1992), some of the laws combine the authority to issue bonds with the procedures necessary to establish the assessment district in the same statute. Others only specify the procedures necessary to establish the assessment district and incorporate by reference another statute for the issuance of the assessment bonds. Thus, from the outset, bond counsel works closely with the city and its financial adviser to determine the financing and manner in which the city elects to secure payment on the principal of the bonds.

3 The Commission will begin considering amendments to the "consultant" regulation (Regulation 18700(a)(2)) at its July meeting. Accordingly, the advice contained in this letter may change as a result of those amendments. Staff invites your comments on the proposed amendments and welcomes your participation in this process.

Bond counsel not only advises the city on compliance with the statutes selected but also prepares all the documents necessary to establish an assessment district. This includes resolutions of intention and confirming the assessment, levying the assessment, proceeding with the assessment, and authorizing the bond issuance. In addition, bond counsel prepares a letter opinion of qualification for the tax exempt status of the bond issuance. Bond counsel drafts the bond covenants in conjunction with the underwriter.

Sometimes, though infrequently, bond counsel may be involved in preparing the terms and provisions of the public works contract and in awarding the contract.

Bond counsel may own property or have an economic interest in the city which may financially benefit by an assessment district proceeding.
sections.analysis:
The Political Reform Act was enacted by the people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The purpose for the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act is to ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed, will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

Section 82048 provides, in pertinent part, that every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency is a "public official." While all designated employees of an agency are deemed to be "public officials" under the Act, this is not necessarily true for consultants. (In re Morrissey (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 120.) Consultants are considered to be "public officials" subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act only if they meet the definition set forth in subdivision (a)(2) of Regulation 18700.

The term "consultant" is defined in Regulation 18700(a)(2) as follows:

"Consultant" shall include any natural person who provides under contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a state or local government agency, provided, however, that "consultant" shall not include a person who:

(A) Conducts research and arrives at conclusions with respect to his or her rendition of information, advice, recommendation, or counsel independent of control and direction of the agency or any agency official, other than normal contract monitoring; and

(B) Possesses no authority with respect to any agency decision beyond the rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel.

Regulation 18700(a)(2).

Thus, a bond counsel is a "consultant," as that term is defined in the Act, if the bond counsel provides information, advice, recommendation or counsel to any state or local government agency and either is subject to the agency's control and direction, or possesses authority with respect to any agency decisions. Conversely, if a bond counsel acts independently of the agency's control and direction, and possesses no authority with respect to any agency decisions beyond the rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel, the bond counsel is not a consultant or public official under the Act. In this regard, note that both of the requirements of Regulation 18700(a)(2), (A) and (B), must be met for the exclusion to operate.

Under subdivision (A) of Regulation 18700(a)(2), the Commission has found that the exclusion does not apply when persons who contract with a government agency ("contractors"), in essence, act like "employees" and provide general advice on an ongoing basis with duties like those normally performed by agency staff. (In re Maloney (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69; In re Leach (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 48; Davis Advice Letter, No. A-91-473.) On the other hand, the exclusion does apply when persons who contract with an agency are retained to use their own judgment and expertise to render professional services according to the specifications of a contract and their decisions are not subject to day-to-day review or direction by the agency. In those situations, the contractors enter into a contract with the public agency to deliver a finished product, just as any vendor of goods and services, and not to participate in or advise the agency on general governmental decisions requiring the subject of their expertise. (Clifford Advice Letter, No. A-83-103.) These persons who contract with an agency do not meet the definition of "consultant" as that term is defined in the Act. Thus, whether a bond counsel comes within the exclusion depends in large part on the degree of the bond counsel's independence from the public agency, in this case, the city.

Under subdivision (B) of Regulation 18700(a)(2), the Commission has determined that the exclusion does not apply when persons who contract with an agency not only offer their professional opinion on specific questions or on requests for information regarding specific factual issues, but also engage in actual governmental decisionmaking, such as participating in meetings and discussions with agency staff. (In re Maloney supra; In re Leach, supra.) Under those circumstances, the contractor's authority may be implied. (Olsen Advice Letter, No. I-91-118.) Furthermore, the definition of consultant does not require that the contractor "make" a final decision. (Nelson Advice Letter, No. I-91-437.)

Thus, Regulation 18700(a)(2) automatically sweeps into the Act any person who has some authority to make an agency decision rather than to merely participate in one by making recommendations to the decisionmaker. Typically, persons who are retained by an agency to perform studies, audits or evaluations are excluded from the coverage of the Act because they do not make governmental decisions and do not act as quasi-employees of the agency. (In re Leach, supra.)

From the inception of Regulation 18700, the Commission has recognized the distinction between the independent contractor and the quasi-employee. In reaching a determination on this vital issue, the Commission has concluded that it is necessary to examine the scope of activities performed by the contractor. The question to ask is whether the duties of the person retained by the public agency are such as to bring them within the meaning of the term "consultant." (Workman Advice Letter, No. I-87-078.)4

It is against this backdrop that we must examine your question. First, it is manifest from your facts that bond counsel

4 For example, in Maloney, the survey-engineer who acted as a consultant for the county was found not to be a public official when he prepared surveys and engineering studies because he was not involved in any official decisionmaking. However, this same person was considered a public official when he acted as a regulator and reviewer of permit applications. (In re Maloney (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69.) When the scope of work of a traffic consultant to a city included traffic studies, providing traffic counts, and identifying necessary street designs and alternative mitigation measures, the Commission concluded that it appeared as though he was an independent contractor and not a consultant as that term is defined in the Act. (Gilbert Advice Letter, No. I-88-441.) However, when the traffic consultant performed other tasks, such as working with city staff to identify trip generation rates for a variety of land uses, assisting the city in determining areas of benefit within the study area, and commenting on alternative financing techniques to distribute costs, the Commission opined that he may be considered a consultant within the meaning of the Act.

does not act independently of the city's control and direction. Bond counsel appears to work closely with city staff and, in fact, functions in an oversight capacity. Thus, the requirement for implementation of the exclusion set forth in subdivision (A) of Regulation 18700(a)(2) has not been met.

Secondly, it is clear from your facts that bond counsel has broad discretion and actively participates in the city's decisionmaking process. Not only does bond counsel participate in the city's initial decision to select the statutes to apply and the method of financing, but bond counsel participates in all city decisions concerning the establishment of the assessment district. In fact, some cities involve the bond counsel in the public works contract. Thus, the requirement for implementation of the exclusion set forth in subdivision (B) of Regulation 18700(a)(2) also has not been met.

In view of the foregoing, we conclude that a person who contracts with a public agency to perform services as a bond counsel is a "consultant" (public official) within the meaning of the Act.

You suggest in your letter that regardless of the definition of "consultant," since bond counsel is retained to provide expertise with respect to a specific project, the issuance of municipal tax-exempt bonds, and not to advise and consult generally, bond counsel should not be treated as a consultant under the Act. The Commission has never enunciated a rule that excludes consultants from coverage under the Act on the basis that they have been retained only for a specific project. Though it is accurate to state that the retention of a consultant under contract for a specific project is a factor we consider in our examination of whether a consultant is a public official under the Act, the Commission has never held that this factor is determinative.5

Despite the fact that bond counsel is retained to provide legal services in connection with only one project, counsel's close working relationship with the city plus counsel's decisionmaking role make it clear that under the facts presented, bond counsel is functioning as a quasi-employee, not as an

5 For an in-depth discussion on this point, we refer you to the Davis Advice Letter, supra at pgs. 9-10, a copy of which is enclosed herewith.)

independent contractor, of the city.6 As such, he is a consultant (public official) under the Act and will have to be designated in the city's conflict of interest code.

is a "consultant" (public official) within the meaning of the Act.3

(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) is a "consultant" (public official) within the meaning of the Act.3 FACTS The City of Chico, like many other public agencies in the state, frequently hires special legal counsel to provide legal services in connection with the issuance of municipal tax-exempt bonds including, but not limited to, general obligation bonds, assessment district bonds, revenue bonds, and Mello-Roos bonds. The services they provide to the agency issuing the bonds consist of legal advice concerning compliance with the procedural statute or ordinance under which the bonds are issued and an opinion, generally relied upon by the municipal bond market, that the bonds being issued by the public agency are, in fact, tax-exempt. Like most city attorneys and other public agency legal counsel, you believe that you have the expertise to independently review advice given by bond counsel in regard to compliance with the particular procedural statute or ordinance under which the bonds are issued. For example, you rely totally on the conclusions of bond counsel to determine whether a bond issuance qualifies as a nontaxable obligation. This type of issuance occurs when a city determines it wants to establish a special assessment to finance public improvements. While plans and specifications for
